17: The designation of Jeconiah (Jehoiachin) as hāʾăsîr — ‘the captive’ or ‘the prisoner’ — in the genealogy (v17) is the Chronicler’s recognition that the Davidic line passed through a deposed and imprisoned king. The word ʾāsîr (prisoner, captive) marks Jeconiah as the king taken to Babylon in 597 BCE (2 Kings 24:8-16) where he remained in prison until Evil-merodach released him in 561 BCE (2 Kings 25:27-30). The Chronicler’s post-exilic Davidic genealogy (vv17-24) continues through this prisoner — Shealtiel, Zerubbabel, and six more generations — demonstrating that the exile and imprisonment of the Davidic king did not sever the covenant line. Zerubbabel (v19) is the key post-exilic Davidic figure, governor of Judah under Persia (Haggai 1:1; Zechariah 4:6-10) and ancestor of Jesus in Luke’s genealogy (Luke 3:27). Matthew 1:11-12 includes Iechonias at the Babylon deportation as the hinge-point of the genealogy — the break and the continuity simultaneously — echoing the Chronicler’s own ambivalent designation: the ʾāsîr through whom the Messiah would come. The word ʾāsîr also carries the resonance of Isaiah 53:8 (meʿōṣer ûmimmišpāṭ luqqāḥ, ‘by oppression and judgment he was taken away’) — the Servant whose suffering is encompassed in the same vocabulary of imprisonment and removal.
1: The list of David’s sons born in Hebron (2 Samuel 3:2-5 is the parallel): Amnon the firstborn by Ahinoam of Jezreel, Daniel the second (2 Samuel 3:3 reads “Chileab” by Abigail of Carmel — the two names may refer to the same person or represent a textual variant; Chileab appears nowhere else in Samuel-Kings narratives, suggesting he died early or lived obscurely, while Daniel is the Chronicler’s form of his name). Amnon’s prominence as firstborn and his later rape of Tamar (2 Sam 13) and murder by Absalom are not recounted here; the genealogy preserves birth order without moral evaluation.
2: The third son: Absalom (ʾaḇšālôm — “father is peace”), son of Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur. The fourth: Adonijah (ʾăḏōnîyyāhû — “YHWH is my lord”), son of Haggith. Both sons became David’s succession rivals: Absalom through armed rebellion (2 Sam 15-18) and Adonijah through attempted usurpation before Solomon’s anointing (1 Kgs 1). The genealogical list preserves the four mothers of David’s Hebron sons (Ahinoam, Abigail, Maacah, Haggith) — each a distinct political alliance, explaining the sons’ different bases of support.
3: The fifth: Shephatiah (šᵉp̄aṭyāh — “YHWH has judged”) by Abital; the sixth: Ithream (yitrᵉʿām — “abundance of people”) by his wife Eglah. The six Hebron sons and their six different mothers document the political complexity of David’s reign in Hebron (c. 1010–1003 BC). Each marriage represented an alliance with a different clan or region. The careful preservation of matrilineal data — unusual in biblical genealogy — reflects the importance of knowing which queen-mother stood behind each potential heir.
4: Six sons born in Hebron; David reigned there seven years and six months. He reigned thirty-three years in Jerusalem. The regnal summary of the Hebron period within the genealogy is unusual — genealogies typically do not incorporate regnal data. Its inclusion here marks the narrative significance of the transition from Hebron to Jerusalem and the corresponding shift in David’s genealogical productivity. The seven-year-six-month Hebron reign and thirty-three-year Jerusalem reign align with the summary in 2 Samuel 5:5 and 1 Kings 2:11.
5: The Jerusalem sons of David by Bathsheba (bat šebaʿ, daughter of Ammiel — 2 Samuel 11:3 reads Eliam son of Ahithophel, which is the patronym Ammiel inverted in Hebrew; Ammiel = my people is God, Eliam = my God is people): Shimea (šimᵉʿāʾ), Shobab, Nathan (nātān), and Solomon (šᵉlōmōh) — four sons. Nathan is the Son of David who appears in Luke’s genealogy of Jesus (Luke 3:31) as opposed to Solomon in Matthew’s (Matthew 1:6). The Chronicler’s listing of Nathan before Solomon (in birth order, as Shimea and Shobab precede both) confirms the separate genealogical lines that produced the two NT genealogical traditions.
6: Nine more sons: Ibhar (yiḇḥār — “he chooses”), Elishama, and Eliphelet. This list and the following verses contain some of the most complex textual issues in Chronicles: Elishama and Eliphelet appear twice in vv6-8, which some scholars attribute to a duplicate source incorporated by the Chronicler, others to two different sons bearing the same name (which does occur in ancient royal households). The parallel in 2 Samuel 5:14-16 and 1 Chronicles 14:4-7 have slightly different lists, suggesting the Chronicler had multiple source traditions.
7: And Nogah (nōgah — “brilliance/shining”), Nepheg (nepeḡ, possibly “sprout/shoot”), and Japhia (yāp̄îaʿ — “shining/radiant”). Nogah does not appear in the parallel 2 Samuel 5:14-16 list; its presence here and in 1 Chronicles 14:6 (but not 2 Samuel) is one of the textual variants between Samuel and Chronicles that may reflect a different source tradition rather than an addition. Nepheg and Japhia are unattested elsewhere outside the royal genealogy lists.
8: And Elishama, Eliada (ʾelyāḏāʿ — “God knows”), and Eliphelet — nine sons. The total of nine additional Jerusalem sons (v6-8), plus the four Bathsheba sons (v5), gives thirteen Jerusalem sons, bringing David’s total named legitimate sons to nineteen (six Hebron + thirteen Jerusalem). The Chronicler notes these are David’s sons “besides the sons of the concubines” (v9), indicating further children whose mothers are not named in the royal registry.
9: All these were David’s sons, besides the sons of the concubines (bᵉnê hāpîlagšîm). Their sister was Tamar (tāmār). Tamar’s name is inserted into the genealogical list — one of very few sisters mentioned in royal genealogy. Her name’s preservation alongside her brothers is a deliberate act of the genealogical tradition, remembering her despite (or because of) the narrative of violation (2 Sam 13) that she endured. The Chronicler records her existence without narrating her story, but the name alone carries its weight for any reader of 2 Samuel.
10: Solomon’s son was Rehoboam (reḥaḇᵉʿām — “the people is enlarged”); his son Abijah (ʾăḇiyyāh — “my father is YHWH”); his son Asa (ʾāsāʾ, possibly “healer”); his son Jehoshaphat (yᵉhôšāp̄āṭ — “YHWH has judged”). The royal genealogy from Solomon to Zedekiah (v10-16) runs through the same kings whose reigns are narrated in 1-2 Kings and 2 Chronicles. The Chronicler’s genealogy presents the succession as a single unbroken chain — the narrative of apostasy and faithfulness compressed into names and succession markers.
11: Joram (yôrām — “YHWH is exalted”) his son, Ahaziah (ʾăḥazyāh — “YHWH has grasped”) his son, Joash (yôʾāš — “YHWH has given/fire of YHWH”) his son. Joram was Jehoshaphat’s son who married Athaliah daughter of Ahab and Jezebel — the most direct intrusion of northern apostasy into the Davidic line. Ahaziah was killed by Jehu (2 Kings 9:27); Joash (the child hidden in the temple, 2 Kgs 11) began a reform that later foundered. The genealogy preserves the succession without noting these crises.
12: Amaziah (ʾămaṣyāh — “YHWH is mighty”) his son, Azariah (ʿăzaryāh — “YHWH has helped”) his son, Jotham (yôtām — “YHWH is perfect/complete”) his son. Azariah is the king called Uzziah in Isaiah, Hosea, Amos, and Zechariah (cf. 2 Kings 15:32; Isaiah 1:1); both names are used in Chronicles and Kings for the same king. His fifty-two-year reign brought prosperity and expansion before his presumptuous entry into the temple led to his leprosy (2 Chronicles 26:16-21). Jotham completed the temple outer gate while his leprous father lived in a separate house.
13: Ahaz (ʾāḥāz — abbreviated from “YHWH has grasped”) his son, Hezekiah (ḥizqiyyāhû — “YHWH strengthens”) his son, Manasseh (mᵉnaššeh — “causing to forget”) his son. The contrast between Ahaz (the king who faced the Syro-Ephraimite coalition and appealed to Assyria rather than YHWH, Isa 7) and his son Hezekiah (who trusted YHWH against Sennacherib) is one of the great theological contrasts in Kings/Chronicles. Manasseh reversed all of Hezekiah’s reforms (2 Kgs 21), though 2 Chr 33 records his captivity and repentance — not mentioned in Kings.
14: Amon (ʾāmôn, possibly related to the Egyptian deity Amun, a detail that fits his apostasy) his son, Josiah (yōʾšiyyāhû — “may YHWH give/sustain”) his son. Amon reigned two years and was assassinated by his own officials (2 Kings 21:23-24). Josiah reversed his father’s apostasy in the greatest reform of any Judahite king, discovering the book of the Torah in the eighteenth year of his reign (2 Kings 22:8). The genealogical succession from apostate Amon to reforming Josiah reflects the covenantal theology of Kings: righteousness is not biologically transmitted but is a divine gift within the Davidic framework.
15: The sons of Josiah: the firstborn Johanan, the second Jehoiakim (yᵉhôyāqîm — “YHWH raises up”), the third Zedekiah (ṣiḏqiyyāhû — “YHWH is righteousness”), the fourth Shallum (šallûm — “recompense/restored”). The textual complexity here is significant: Johanan (the firstborn) does not appear in the Kings succession narrative. Shallum is identified in Jeremiah 22:11 as “Shallum son of Josiah, who reigned in place of his father Josiah” — making Shallum the same as Jehoahaz (2 Kings 23:30-34), the fourth-born who was chosen king first. The Chronicler preserves birth order (Johanan first, Shallum fourth) while the historical succession followed popular choice rather than primogeniture.
16: The sons of Jehoiakim: Jeconiah (yᵉḵonyāh — also Coniah in Jeremiah 22:24; Jehoiachin in Kings) his son, and Zedekiah his son. The “Zedekiah his son” here (Jehoiakim’s son) is distinct from Josiah’s son Zedekiah (v15) — this is either a confusion in the text or represents two distinct individuals sharing the name. Jeconiah (= Jehoiachin) reigned three months before the first Babylonian deportation (597 BC); Jeremiah’s oracle about Coniah (Jeremiah 22:30 — “write this man down as childless”) is theologically resolved by Matthew’s genealogy, where Jesus descends through Jeconiah but receives the throne through divine adoption rather than biological succession.
18: And Malchiram (malḵîrām — “my king is exalted”), Pedaiah (pᵉḏāyāh — “YHWH has ransomed”), Shenazzar, Jekamiah (yᵉqamyāh — “YHWH establishes”), Hoshama (hôšāmāʿ — “YHWH has heard”), and Nedabiah (nᵉḏaḇyāh — “YHWH is willing/noble”). Seven sons of Jeconiah in exile (v17-18). The names include Hebrew theophoric names alongside Shenazzar, which is probably a Babylonian name (šin-ab-uṣur — “Sin protect the father” — cognate with Sheshbazzar, another early leader of the return). The combination of Hebrew and Babylonian names reflects the cultural reality of the Babylonian exile.
19: The sons of Pedaiah: Zerubbabel (zᵉrubbāḇel — “seed/shoot of Babylon” or “descendant born in Babylon”) and Shimei. The sons of Zerubbabel: Meshullam and Hananiah. Their sister was Shelomith. Zerubbabel is identified elsewhere as son of Shealtiel (Haggai 1:1; Matthew 1:12; Luke 3:27) rather than son of Pedaiah here. The discrepancy is usually resolved by levirate marriage: Pedaiah may have married his dead brother Shealtiel’s wife, making Zerubbabel biologically Pedaiah’s son but legally Shealtiel’s. Zerubbabel led the first return from exile (Ezra 2:2; 1 Chronicles 3:2) and supervised the rebuilding of the Second Temple foundation.
20: And Hashubah (ḥăšûḇāh — “YHWH has considered”), Ohel (ʾōhel — “tent”), Berechiah (bᵉreḵyāh — “YHWH blesses”), Hasadiah (ḥăsaḏyāh — “YHWH is loyal/gracious”), and Jushabhesed (yûšaḇḥeseḏ — “loving kindness is restored”, a theologically dense name) — five more. Zerubbabel’s sons include a remarkable name in Jushabhesed: “loving kindness/covenant loyalty is restored” is a name expressing hope for the restoration of YHWH’s ḥeseḏ in the post-exilic period — a theological statement in a name, consistent with the expectation that the Davidic line’s continuation through Zerubbabel signals YHWH’s ongoing covenantal commitment.
21: The sons of Hananiah: Pelatiah (pᵉlaṭyāh — “YHWH has saved/escaped”) and Jeshaiah (yᵉšaʿyāh — “YHWH is salvation”). Then the son of Rephaiah (rᵉp̄āyāh — “YHWH heals”), the son of Arnan (ʾarnān), the son of Obadiah (ʿōḇaḏyāh — “servant of YHWH”), the son of Shechaniah (šᵉḵanyāh — “YHWH dwells”). The list here is difficult syntactically — the repeated “son of” constructions have been read as a list of individuals (some MT reading) or as a genealogical chain (four consecutive generations). The grammatical ambiguity reflects either the difficulty of the source material or intentional genealogical compression.
22: The sons of Shechaniah: Shemaiah (šᵉmaʿyāh — “YHWH has heard”). The sons of Shemaiah: Hattush, Igal, Bariah, Neariah, and Shaphat — six. These six sons of Shemaiah complete the Shechaniah branch. Hattush appears in Ezra 8:2 as one of the leaders of the second return from exile under Artaxerxes — providing a chronological anchor for the genealogy’s endpoint. The Chronicler’s genealogy thus extends from David’s Hebron sons through the Babylonian exile and into the Persian-period return, connecting the Davidic promise to the post-exilic community.
23: The sons of Neariah: Elioenai (ʾelyôʿênay — “my eyes toward YHWH” — an unusual name expressing prayerful dependence), Hizkiah (ḥizqiyyāh — a short form of Hezekiah, “YHWH strengthens”), and Azrikam (ʿazrîqām — “my help has risen”) — three. The post-exilic names in this generation continue the theophoric pattern of hope and restoration: “eyes toward YHWH” expresses the posture of prayerful waiting that characterized the community of the return.
24: The sons of Elioenai: Hodaviah (hôḏawyāhû — “praise YHWH”), Eliashib (ʾelyāšîḇ — “God restores”), Pelaiah (pᵉlāyāh — “YHWH is wonderful”), Akkub (ʿaqqûḇ, possibly “protected by YHWH”), Johanan (yôḥānān — “YHWH is gracious”), Delaiah (dᵉlāyāhû — “YHWH has drawn up/lifted”), and Anani (ʿănānî — “YHWH is my cloud/presence”) — seven. The genealogy ends with seven sons of the last named generation, continuing approximately three or four generations beyond Zerubbabel. This places the genealogy’s endpoint in the early Persian period (c. 500-450 BC), consistent with the Chronicler’s likely date of composition. The name Anani (“my cloud”) as the final name in the Davidic genealogy resonates with the cloud of divine presence that accompanied Israel through the wilderness and filled Solomon’s temple — the genealogy ends with a name pointing toward the return of YHWH’s presence to his people.
